perturbation.original_text: Upon written demand of the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party shall cease using the Confidential Source: RAE SYSTEMS INC, 10-Q, 11/14/2000 Information and return the Confidential Information and all copies, notes or extracts thereof to the Disclosing Party within seven (7) days of receipt of notice.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon written demand of the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party shall cease using the Confidential Source: RAE SYSTEMS INC, 10-Q, 11/14/2000 Information and return the Confidential Information and all copies, notes or extracts thereof to the Disclosing Party within three (3) days of receipt of notice.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the timeframe for returning confidential information to three days may be insufficient and contradict reasonable timeframes allowed under California law, especially if a large amount of information needs to be compiled and returned.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure governs discovery and the need for reasonable timeframes. While this section pertains to discovery requests, the principle of allowing reasonable time for compliance extends to contractual obligations. A three-day turnaround may be deemed unreasonable, especially in cases with voluminous or complex information, potentially leading to legal challenges based on insufficient time for compliance.

MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 4. CIVIL DISCOVERY ACT [2016.010 - 2036.050] ( Title 4 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) CHAPTER 14. Inspection, Copying, Testing, Sampling, and Production of Documents, Electronically Stored Information, Tangible Things, Land, and Other Property [2031.010 - 2031.510] ( Heading of Chapter 14 amended by Stats. 2012, Ch. 72, Sec. 30. ) ARTICLE 2. Response to Inspection Demand [2031.210 - 2031.320] ( Article 2 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) 2031.280. (a) Any documents or category of documents produced in response to a demand for inspection, copying, testing, or sampling shall be identified with the specific request number to which the documents respond. (b) The documents shall be produced on the date specified in the demand pursuant to paragraph (2) of subdivision (c) of Section 2031.030, unless an objection has been made to that date. If the date for inspection has been extended pursuant to Section 2031.270, the documents shall be produced on the date agreed to pursuant to that section. perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the timeframe for returning confidential information from seven days to three days. The Perturbation Explanation suggests this timeframe may be insufficient under California law, and the Law Explanation further explains that California law requires 'reasonable timeframes.' Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 pertains to discovery requests under CCP 2031.280, the Law Explanation extends the 'principle of allowing reasonable time for compliance' to contractual obligations. A three-day deadline, if deemed unreasonable, could lead to legal challenges, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: SpinRecords.com shall maintain such books of account and records which support each statement for at least two years after the termination or expiration of this contract or after the final payment made by SpinRecords.com to NETTAXI, whichever is later.
perturbation.changed_text: SpinRecords.com shall maintain such books of account and records which support each statement for at least one year after the termination or expiration of this contract or after the final payment made by SpinRecords.com to NETTAXI, whichever is later.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the record retention period to one year may violate IRS guidelines and California state requirements for maintaining business records.
perturbation.law_explanation: 26 U.S. Code § 6001 grants the IRS authority to create record-keeping requirements. While the IRS generally recommends retaining records for three years from when a return is filed (if tax issues are involved), California may have parallel state requirements for maintaining supporting documentation for business transactions. Reducing the retention period to one year can potentially expose the parties to penalties if they are unable to produce records during an audit or legal dispute falling outside this one-year window.

Notice or regulations requiring records, statements, and special returns Every person liable for any tax imposed by this title, or for the collection thereof, shall keep such records, render such statements, make such
perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the record retention period from two years to one year. The Law Explanation indicates that reducing the retention period can expose parties to penalties if records are needed for audits or legal disputes beyond the one-year window. 26 U.S. Code § 6001, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, grants the IRS authority to create record-keeping requirements. The original contract adheres to a longer retention period which aligns with the IRS's general recommendation of three years, while the changed contract does not. Thus, the change creates a potential legal contradiction by not meeting the potential record-keeping requirements of the IRS.
